comment.body:
And another cut in September.

Just remember, in any rate cutting cycle, the FED does not do so when the market is rallying.

It usually cuts as the market begins to decline as a leading indicator. SPX has traded lower than where it was when the FED began its first cut last year in September.

They also began cutting before the Covid crash as well.

So more cuts is actually bad news.

And remember, SPX rallied while the FED was hiking in 23’ and continued to rally as the Funds rate sat at 5.5% for almost all of 24’.

SPX also rallied while the Fed was hiking in 2018.

comment.body:
My 2 cents on how the FOMC can be a “bullish event”. Which I am gathering from the chat that it’s hard to define a bullish outcome based on what the Fed (cut plans) may be. 

My take - and I don’t watch cnbc and don’t follow any furus so this is a highly uneducated  guess - and could also be redundant to what the financial news is saying…. is that if JP provides a calming effect on the market that’s all that’s needed to turn it into a bullish event 

That means saying  

1. They remain data dependent (consistency is calming?

2. Data shows progress on inflation and also gives a nod to how Trump and doge cutting back on fiscal spending (including govt layoffs) is driving the drop in inflation 

3. And at the same time sees a soft landing being possible with the help of the Fed by easing rates (the dot plot may back that up but if he just says something that explicit the algos will go crazy). This also takes the focus off of how many cuts they’re doing and what does it signal about the economy if they plan too many cuts or too few 

Again in just brainstorming what could be a bullish scenario

comment.body:
But it’s not like we hit 2% when the rate decreases started. 

What caused the selloff in December Post-FOMC (SPY -2.8%), was the FED being concerned with persistent inflation and a stronger labor market, and JP suggesting that monetary policy might remain tighter for longer.  

My guess is that with latest inflation numbers and current unemployment forecasts his tune will start to shift towards looser monetary policy and hinting at potential QE if the trends continue.
